title: Introduction to Python

introduction.what_it_is: Python is a high-level, interpreted, general-purpose programming language created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991. Its design philosophy emphasizes code readability with the use of significant indentation. Python is dynamically typed and garbage-collected, supporting multiple programming paradigms including procedural, object-oriented, and functional programming.
introduction.why_important: Python has become one of the most popular programming languages in the world due to its simplicity and versatility. It significantly reduces the cost of program maintenance through its easily readable syntax. Furthermore, its massive standard library and active open-source community provide tools and frameworks for almost any task.
real_world: Data Science and Machine Learning (Netflix recommendation algorithms)

detailed_theory:
When you write a Python program, you don't need to compile it into machine code before executing it. Instead, you run the code directly through the Python interpreter. The interpreter reads the code line by line, translates it into intermediate byte code, and executes it. This makes the development cycle incredibly fast. 

Python was designed to be highly extensible. Rather than having all of its functionality built into its core, it relies on modules and packages. This means the core language remains small and easy to learn, but it can be expanded endlessly. 

One of the most notable features of Python is its use of whitespace. While languages like C++ or Java use curly braces `{}` to define code blocks, Python uses indentation. This enforces a visually clean coding style and prevents the common problem of mismatched brackets. 

Python is 'dynamically typed', meaning you don't have to declare the type of a variable when you create one. The type is determined at runtime based on the value assigned to it. While this offers flexibility and speed of development, it requires developers to be mindful of types during execution to avoid runtime errors.

features.name: Readable Syntax
features.description: Python code reads almost like plain English, making it incredibly beginner-friendly and maintainable.

features.name: Interpreted Language
features.description: Code is executed line by line, which makes debugging easier and eliminates the compilation step.

features.name: Extensive Standard Library
features.description: Comes 'batteries included' with modules for file I/O, networking, web scraping, and more out of the box.

features.name: Dynamically Typed
features.description: No need to define variable types explicitly; the interpreter handles it at runtime.

advantages: Extremely gentle learning curve compared to C++ or Java.
Strong community support and abundant documentation.
limitations: Slower execution speed compared to compiled languages like C or Rust.
High memory consumption due to flexibility of data types.
Not traditionally used for mobile app development or high-end game development.

syntax.explanation: Python's syntax relies heavily on standard English keywords rather than punctuation. It uses newlines to complete a command (as opposed to semicolons) and indentation (spaces or tabs) to define scope (such as loops, functions, and classes).
breakdown: Line 1: `name = 'Alice'` - Assigns the string 'Alice' to the variable `name`.
Line 2: `if name == 'Alice':` - Starts a conditional block. The colon `:` is required.
Line 3: `    print('Hello Alice')` - This line is indented by 4 spaces, indicating it belongs to the `if` block.
rules: Always use 4 spaces for indentation (do not mix tabs and spaces).
End structural statements (if, for, def, class) with a colon `:`.
Variables are case-sensitive (`Age` is different from `age`).

examples.title: Hello World Program
examples.explanation: The `print()` function simply outputs the string passed into it to the console. This is typically the first program written when learning a new language.

examples.title: Simple Condition and Input
examples.output:
Enter your name: Alice
Welcome, Alice!
examples.explanation: This example shows how to take user input using `input()`, store it in a variable, and use an `if/else` statement to conditionally execute code. The `f'...'` syntax is an f-string, allowing easy variable interpolation.

examples.title: A Simple Function with Type Hinting
examples.explanation: This shows defining a function `def`, using type hints (`: float` and `-> float`) to indicate expected types, importing a built-in module `math`, performing mathematical exponentiation `**`, and formatting a float to 2 decimal places `:.2f`.

examples.title: Fetching Data from an API
examples.code:
import urllib.request
import json

url = 'https://api.github.com/users/octocat'
response = urllib.request.urlopen(url)
data = json.loads(response.read())
print(f"User: {data['name']}, Followers: {data['followers']}")
examples.explanation: This simulates a real-world scenario where Python is used to make an HTTP request to an API, parse the JSON response, and extract relevant data. This is foundational for backend web development and data engineering.

comparisons.explanation: Compiled languages translate the entire source code into machine code before execution, resulting in an executable file. This makes them fast to run but slower to build. Interpreted languages translate and execute code line-by-line simultaneously. This makes execution slower, but development and debugging much faster.

methods.name: print()
methods.description: Prints the specified message to the screen, or other standard output device.

methods.name: input()
methods.description: Reads a line from input (usually the user via console), converts it to a string, and returns it.

methods.name: type()
methods.description: Returns the type of the specified object.

mistakes.title: IndentationError
mistakes.description: Mixing tabs and spaces or using inconsistent numbers of spaces.
mistakes.debugging_tip: Configure your text editor or IDE to automatically convert tabs to 4 spaces.

mistakes.title: Missing Colons
mistakes.description: Forgetting the colon at the end of a control flow statement.
mistakes.debugging_tip: If you get a SyntaxError immediately following an `if`, `for`, `while`, or `def` statement, check for a missing colon.

basic.question: What is Python?
basic.answer: Python is a high-level, interpreted, general-purpose programming language known for its readability and simplicity.

basic.question: Is Python interpreted or compiled?
basic.answer: Python is an interpreted language. The source code is compiled into bytecode under the hood, which is then interpreted by the Python Virtual Machine (PVM).

intermediate.question: What is PEP 8?
intermediate.answer: PEP 8 is the official style guide for Python code. It provides conventions on how to format code (e.g., using 4 spaces for indentation, variable naming conventions) to ensure readability and consistency across the Python community.

intermediate.question: How is memory managed in Python?
intermediate.answer: Python uses automatic memory management through a private heap containing all objects. It employs a built-in garbage collector that reclaims memory from objects that are no longer referenced (using reference counting and cyclic garbage collection).

advanced.question: What is the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL)?
advanced.answer: The GIL is a mutex in CPython that protects access to Python objects, preventing multiple native threads from executing Python bytecodes at once. This makes CPython thread-safe but prevents true multi-threading for CPU-bound tasks.

easy: Write a Python script that prints your name and age on two separate lines.
How do you comment a single line of code in Python?
What function is used to take user input?
Explain the significance of indentation in Python.
Which symbol is used to assign a value to a variable?
medium: Write a script that takes a user's birth year as input and calculates their age.
Explain the difference between Python 2 and Python 3 briefly.
What is a dynamically typed language?
How does Python run code without explicit compilation by the user?
Create a variable `x` with the value 5, and print its type.
hard: Explain the concept of garbage collection in Python.
Discuss the pros and cons of Python's execution speed compared to C++.
What are `.pyc` files and when are they created?
Explain how you would run a Python script from the command line passing arguments to it.
Write a small script using `sys.argv` to print command line arguments.

coding_challenges.statement: Write a program that asks the user for their name, and then greets them with 'Hello, [name]! Welcome to Python.'
coding_challenges.solution:
name = input('Enter your name: ')
print(f'Hello, {name}! Welcome to Python.')

real_world_applications.explanation: Frameworks like Django and Flask allow developers to build complex, scalable backend APIs and web applications rapidly.
Libraries like Pandas, NumPy, and Matplotlib make Python the undisputed king of data manipulation, analysis, and visualization.
TensorFlow, PyTorch, and Scikit-learn provide the foundation for building advanced AI models and neural networks.
Python scripts are widely used by System Administrators and DevOps engineers to automate repetitive tasks, manage server configurations, and build CI/CD pipelines.

best_practices: Follow PEP 8 guidelines for code styling to ensure your code is readable by other developers.
Use meaningful and descriptive variable names (e.g., `user_age` instead of `x`).
Keep your functions short and focused on a single task (Single Responsibility Principle).
Write comments to explain *why* code does something, not *what* it does (the code itself should show what it does).
summary: Python is an interpreted, high-level language focusing on readability.
It uses indentation instead of braces to define code blocks.
It is dynamically typed, meaning variable types are inferred at runtime.
Python has a massive standard library and third-party ecosystem.
It is widely used in Web Dev, Data Science, AI, and Automation.

quiz.question: Who created Python?
quiz.explanation: Python was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991.

quiz.question: Which of the following is correct regarding Python's typing system?
quiz.explanation: Python does not require variable types to be explicitly declared before assignment; the interpreter infers the type at runtime.

quiz.question: What is the correct extension for a Python source file?
quiz.explanation: All Python source code files should end with the `.py` extension.

quiz.question: Which character is used to indicate a single-line comment in Python?
quiz.explanation: The `#` symbol tells the Python interpreter to ignore the rest of the line.

quiz.question: How do you define a block of code in Python?
quiz.explanation: Python uses whitespace indentation (usually 4 spaces) to define the scope of code blocks like loops and functions.

quiz.question: Which built-in function is used to display output on the screen?
quiz.explanation: `print()` is the standard output function in Python.

quiz.question: What is the official style guide for Python code called?
quiz.explanation: PEP 8 (Python Enhancement Proposal 8) outlines the best practices for formatting Python code.

quiz.question: Is Python case-sensitive?
quiz.explanation: Python treats `Variable` and `variable` as two entirely different identifiers.

quiz.question: Which of these is NOT a primary use case for Python?
quiz.explanation: OS Kernels are typically written in lower-level languages like C or Rust for absolute memory control and speed. Python is too high-level and slow for kernel development.

quiz.question: What happens to the Python source code before it is executed?
options: It is compiled to machine code.
It is converted to intermediate bytecode.
It is translated to C code.
Nothing, it is read entirely as raw text.
quiz.answer: It is converted to intermediate bytecode.
quiz.explanation: The interpreter compiles the `.py` source code into hidden bytecode (sometimes saved as `.pyc`), which is then executed by the Python Virtual Machine.
